title: Maqluba
subtitle: Palestinian upside-down rice with chicken and aubergine
excerpt: A celebratory Palestinian dish: spiced basmati rice layered with fried aubergine, tomato, and chicken pieces, packed into a deep pot, cooked until the rice is done, and then dramatically inverted onto a large platter so the layered base becomes the top. 'Maqluba' means 'upside-down' in Arabic.
sourceNotes: Maqluba is considered the national dish of Palestine and is also eaten in Jordan, Syria, and Lebanon. It is documented in Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) and in Sami Tamimi and Yotam Ottolenghi's 'Jerusalem' (2012). The dish is typically made for gatherings and family celebrations. The technique of cooking the components in a pot and inverting to serve produces a dramatic presentation.

content.text: Maqluba requires a heavy-based pot with a tight-fitting lid (a standard large saucepan works well). The components are assembled in layers from the bottom up: tomato slices, fried aubergine, cooked chicken pieces, then the spiced rice. During cooking the layers fuse together and the rice absorbs the chicken stock and aubergine oil. The 15-minute rest after cooking is not optional. It allows the rice to firm up and the steam to redistribute, which is what makes the inversion possible.

To invert: place a large flat plate or board over the top of the pot, hold both firmly together, and flip in one decisive movement. Tap the base of the pot, then lift it off slowly. If some rice sticks, scrape it out and pack it back into position, the crispy rice stuck to the base is the tahdig layer of the dish and considered the best part.

items.name: Basmati rice
items.prepNote: rinsed and soaked in cold water for 30 minutes

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: 1 quartered for the stock, 1 finely chopped

content.text: Place the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces in a saucepan with the quartered {{onion}}, 1 litre of water, and half the spice mix. Bring to the boil, skim, then simmer for 25 minutes until the chicken is just cooked. Remove the chicken, strain and reserve 700 ml of the stock.

Season the {{aubergine}} slices with salt. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large frying pan over high heat and fry the aubergine in batches for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden. Drain on kitchen paper.

In the same pot used for the chicken, brush with a little oil. Arrange the {{tomato}} slices in a single layer on the base. Layer the fried {{aubergine}} on top, then the chicken pieces, skin-side down. Drain the soaked {{basmati-rice}} and toss with the remaining spice mix and salt. Pour the rice evenly over the chicken, pressing it down gently. Pour over the reserved chicken stock. It should come just to the top of the rice. Bring to the boil, then cover tightly and cook over the lowest possible heat for 25 minutes. Remove from the heat and rest, covered, for 15 minutes.

Run a butter knife around the edge of the pot. Place a large platter over the top and invert decisively in one movement. Leave for 30 seconds, then lift the pot. Scatter toasted {{pine-nuts}} over the top if using. Serve with plain yoghurt and a simple salad.

items.fix: 15 minutes resting is the minimum, the rice needs to firm up and the steam to dissipate. If very wet when you rest it, leave the lid slightly ajar for the last 5 minutes
items.cause: Not enough resting time or rice too wet

items.fix: The lowest possible heat setting after bringing to the boil. A heat diffuser helps if your hob runs hot
The stock should just cover the rice when added. If the lid is loose, weigh it down or seal with foil before putting the lid on
items.cause: Too little stock or lid not tight enough

items.fix: Salting draws out moisture and the high-heat fry creates a crust that keeps the aubergine from breaking down during the rice cooking stage
items.cause: Not fried at high enough heat or not salted first

content.text: Maqluba is one of the great dishes of Palestinian cooking. It is made for celebrations, family gatherings, and weddings. It is brought to the table and turned out in front of guests for the drama of it. The name means 'upside-down'. The word fits both the method and the pleasure of the dish. It hides its layers until the moment of serving. Then it reveals a tower of rice, aubergine, and meat. It is claimed as a national dish in Palestine and in Jordan. It is also made in Lebanon and Syria. This is the shared Levantine food culture of the region.
